setting.note: Greene's Saigon is the French colonial capital in its last years, seen through the weary British correspondent Fowler and the earnest young American Pyle. The map holds the short, dense spine of the old centre: the terrace of the Continental, the shopfronts of the rue Catinat, the square by the opera house where a bomb goes off at mid-morning, and the Majestic on the river where the war and the city meet the water. It brings Vietnam and 1950s Saigon into the atlas.

rights.originalWork: In copyright. Graham Greene, first published 1955. This book is mapped as a cited work: we store none of its text and reproduce no passages. The places are facts and the notes are our own.
rights.textSource: None stored. A cited book reproduces no text.
rights.territoryNotes: Locations and original commentary only. No reproduction of the copyrighted text, in any territory.
rights.attribution: The Quiet American, Graham Greene (1955). Locations mapped by Read the Places; not affiliated with or endorsed by the author or publisher.

palette.note: The dull red of the colonial shutters along the rue Catinat and of the violence that keeps breaking through the surface calm. A cited book's colour is not a quotation, because we store no text to quote; it still has to be legible, and this clears WCAG AA on paper.
